gr11-basque-country-navarre.heading: Green hills before the high cols
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: This is the Atlantic end of the GR11, where the walking is rolling rather than alpine: ridges and meadows between caseríos, with the day's work coming from accumulated up-and-down over long stages rather than altitude. From Cabo Higuer at sea level the route climbs gradually inland, crossing low passes — Lizarrieta, Urkiaga, Argibiela — before the ground steepens and rises into the Roncal valley. By the col into Aragón at Zuriza you are above 1,200 m, and the real climbs are about to begin.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.heading: Beech forest most of the way
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: European beech shades stage after stage here, from the Quinto Real woods above Elizondo to the long descent into Isaba. East of Hiriberri the route skirts the Irati Forest — the second-largest and best-preserved beech-fir forest in Europe, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve since 2024, where silver fir joins the beech and oak, birch and willow fill the gaps. In autumn the holly and ferns come into their own; the higher passes give way to open pasture.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.heading: Baztan, shepherds & witches
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: You cross into Navarre on the first day and spend the stretch in deep Basque country — euskera place names, stone caseríos, and the bordas (shepherds' huts) of the Quinto Real and Aezkoa valleys. Elizondo is the capital of the Baztan, the valley whose neighbour Zugarramurdi gave the Inquisition its most famous witch trials between 1609 and 1614. Ochagavía and Isaba keep the older rhythm of the Salazar and Roncal valleys, where transhumant shepherding shaped the high pastures the trail crosses.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.heading: The wet, green end of the range
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: This is the Atlantic flank of the Pyrenees, closest to the Bay of Biscay, and it is green because it is wet — cloud and rain arrive off the sea more often than further east. Fog is the practical hazard, not heat: several stages warn that mist in the upper pasture zones, between Urkiaga and the Abodi passes, can make orientation hard. The walking season runs roughly March to November, with summer the most settled.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.heading: Flysch gives way to granite
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: The early stages cut across the folded flysch and limestone of the Inner Sierras — alternating sandstone and marl that bends into shapes like the Paso de la Oreja's anticline above Lizara. By the Tena valley the rock turns harder and the ground steps up into true high country: glacier-carved cirques, ibones (mountain lakes) like Anayet and Bachimaña, and passes above 2,700 m at Tebarray and Brazato. This is the first stretch where the trail stays high for days rather than dropping to a village each night.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.heading: Through the pine line into open meadow
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: Below about 1,600 m you walk through beech and silver fir; higher up the subalpine belt is pino negro (Pinus uncinata), the mountain pine that holds the slopes up to roughly 2,400 m, often over a carpet of rhododendron and bilberry. Cross the treeline — around 2,200–2,450 m here — and the forest gives out to alpine meadow and juniper, the grazed grassland that fills Aguas Tuertas and the high pastures. The meadows are at their fullest and most flowered in the weeks just after snowmelt.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.heading: A valley run on grass
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: The Tena valley, with Sallent de Gállego as its main town, was for centuries organised around its pastures — managed by the Junta General de la Val de Tena and split into the quiñones of Sallent, Panticosa and Partacua. Pyrenean life has mingled with pastoralism for six or seven thousand years, flocks climbing to the high meadows in late spring and coming down in autumn. The grazed grass underfoot at Aguas Tuertas and Respomuso is the working surface of that history, not a backdrop to it.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.heading: Be over the pass before the build-up
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: Summer days are hot with hard sun, but in July and August thunderstorms build through the afternoon, especially when a southerly wind pushes moist air up from the plains — the standard rule is to be off exposed ridges and high passes before 2 pm. Snow lingers on the highest cols well into summer: Tebarray (2,771 m) can hold it until mid-August, and Brazato into July, both steep enough to want an ice axe when iced. Start early, and treat the high passes as the part of the day that decides the rest.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.heading: Limestone cut by water
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: This is karst country: the Ordesa canyon was carved by the río Arazas into Cretaceous and Eocene limestone, and the same rock holds Europe's deepest gorges on the Spanish side. Above them sits Monte Perdido, whose north-face glacier is one of the southernmost in Europe and retreating fast. The walking ranges from forested valley floors to high passes above 2,500m, including the drop off Collado de Añisclo into Pineta — roughly 1,200m of descent in about 2.5km, the steepest sustained loss on the GR11.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.heading: Beech below, endemics on the walls
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: Climbing the Ordesa valley you pass through the Bosque de las Hayas, a beech forest, before the trees give way to black pine and then open alpine grassland higher up. The park holds over 1,500 plant species, and the limestone cliffs and scree shelter Pyrenean endemics found almost nowhere else — among them Ramonda myconi and Borderea pyrenaica. The richest displays cling to the rock walls and ledges rather than the path itself.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.heading: Clear mornings, building afternoons
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: In July and August the pattern is reliable enough to plan around: clear mornings, then thunderstorms building in the afternoon when warm, moist air pushes up from the south. The high passes here — Chistau at 2,592m, Vallibierna at 2,710m — are exposed and dangerous in a storm. Start early, aim to be off the cols by midday, and treat dark clouds or thunder as a signal to drop down rather than push on.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.heading: Lakeside grass to scree and pine
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: Around Restanca, Colomèrs and Sant Maurici the high basins hold alpine meadow and lakeside grass below bare granite, with the Els Encantats outcrop standing over Sant Maurici. The descent into Pallars Sobirà passes through pine woodland and old terraced pastures around villages like Estaon and Boldís, where abandoned barns mark land once grazed hard. Toward Malniu and the Cerdanya the ground turns drier and scrubbier as Mediterranean influence creeps in from the south.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.heading: Pastoral Pallars, then Andorra's two faces
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: The valley stages thread Pallars Sobirà villages — Espot, Estaon, Tavascan, Àreu — past summer barns and half-empty hamlets that record a pastoral economy now largely gone. Andorra shows two faces in quick succession: the ski-resort development of Arinsal and Encamp, then the Madriu-Perafita-Claror valley, a UNESCO World Heritage cultural landscape (2004) where summer settlements, stone tracks and a communal land system reaching back to the 13th century survive. Refugi de l'Illa sits inside that protected valley.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.heading: A July–September window, snow lingers high
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: This stretch walks best from July to September, when the huts are staffed and the high ports are clear. In early summer snow holds on the highest cols — Port de Ratera (2,534m) and especially Port de Baiau (2,757m), where an ice axe and crampons can be needed and the boulder slope above is loose. Later in the season the lower valleys around Tavascan and the approach to Malniu turn hot and dry, so the risk shifts from snow to heat and water.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.heading: From the border ridge to the sea
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: This stretch loses the high Pyrenees in stages. Above Núria the GR11 still walks the frontier crest from the Collado de Noucreus past peaks near 2,800m, but past Ulldeter it drops into the green Ripollès valleys and then the lower Alta Garrotxa, before the Albera range carries it down to the Mediterranean at Cap de Creus. The last day finishes barely above sea level on wind-scoured rock, a long way in feel from the alpine cols where the section began.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.heading: Pine, cork oak, then scrub
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: The vegetation tracks the descent. Steep pine forest above Planoles and Queralbs gives way to mixed mountain woodland around Molló and the dense Mediterranean woods of the Alta Garrotxa, then to the cork oak and pine of the Albera range above Requessens. By Cap de Creus the trees are gone, replaced by low wind-pruned scrub on the rock — the tramuntana shapes what grows as much as it shapes the stone.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.heading: Sanctuaries, monasteries and the Cerdanya
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: The walking threads a chain of religious sites: the mountain sanctuary of Núria, reached on foot or by the Cremallera rack railway running up from Ribes de Freser since 1931; the Romanesque chapel of Sant Aniol d'Aguja, with a monastery founded in 859; and Sant Pere de Rodes, a Catalan Romanesque monastery on the Serra de Rodes whose church was consecrated in 1022. Puigcerdà sits in the broad Cerdanya, a wide upland valley split between Spain and France.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.heading: Snow up high, tramuntana down low
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: The legs from Molló east to Cap de Creus can be walked year-round, but the high stages around Núria and Ulldeter hold snow from roughly mid-December into late June, where an ice axe and crampons can be needed on the ridge. Lower down the danger flips from cold to heat and water: stages like Talaixà to Albanyà list heat as the main difficulty. On the coast the tramuntana, a northwest wind that can reach gale force, dominates the final approach.

gr11-s1.heading: Overview
gr11-s1.body: The trail starts at Cabo Higuer with the Cantabrian Sea below, then climbs gradually inland through Hondarribia and Irún and over the Ursain col to the Endara reservoir before dropping to Bera in Navarre. It is a long day — around 30 km — and the one thing that matters is route-finding through the streets of Hondarribia and Irún; once you are out of town the waymarking is excellent.

gr11-s2.heading: Overview
gr11-s2.body: A long stage of nearly 30 km that strings together a series of low cols — Lizarrieta among them — across meadows and past stone caseríos before descending to Elizondo in the Baztan valley. There is no single hard climb; the day's difficulty is the sheer accumulated up-and-down, so pace it and use the bar-shop at the Lizarrieta pass.

gr11-s3.heading: Overview
gr11-s3.body: From Elizondo the route leaves the Atlantic hills and climbs steadily into the beech forests of the Quinto Real, a gradual ascent rather than a steep one, to the Puerto de Urkiaga. Plan your night before you set off — Urkiaga itself has no accommodation, with the nearest beds at Eugi, well off the trail.

gr11-s4.heading: Overview
gr11-s4.body: A walk through the heart of the Quinto Real, climbing past bordas and hunting posts through old beech woods that open into broad pasture at the high point before the drop to Burguete. Watch the open upper sections in fog, where the grassland gives few handrails.

gr11-s5.heading: Overview
gr11-s5.body: An easy 17 km on tracks and forest paths, through beech then pine over the Usateguieta col and down into the Aezkoa valley, finishing across the Irati river on a medieval bridge into Hiriberri. A gentle day and a good one to recover legs after the cols.

gr11-s6.heading: Overview
gr11-s6.body: The route skirts the edge of the Irati Forest and climbs over the open Sierra de Abodi — Ollokiate and Tapla passes, the Abodi vertex at nearly 1,500 m — before a steep descent past the Muskilda sanctuary into Ochagavía. The high traverse is exposed, so the thing to watch is fog between the passes; otherwise the views run the length of the Pyrenees.

gr11-s7.heading: Overview
gr11-s7.body: A mostly track-walking day from the Salazar valley to the Roncal, leisurely through pine and box until the land opens below Kakueta and the peaks come into view, then a steep beech-forest descent that tests the knees into Isaba. Carry water — there are no fountains until almost the end of the stage.

gr11-s8.heading: Overview
gr11-s8.body: A short 11 km half-day that leaves Isaba up the Belabarze ravine through thick forest, eases into cattle pasture, then crosses the Collado de Argibiela (Puerto de los Navarros) to leave Navarre for Aragón and descend gently to Zuriza. Note that the Zuriza campsite is closed, though a free two-floor shelter remains below it.

gr11-s9.heading: Overview
gr11-s9.body: A sustained climb out of Zuriza up the Petraficha ravine to its col at 1,963 m, then down through the Aragón Subordán valley and up into the meadow at Aguas Tuertas. The night here is a tiny free cabin sleeping four or five with no services, so carry food, mat and a bag — and plan for the next day too, as resupply is thin until Sallent de Gállego.

gr11-s10.heading: Overview
gr11-s10.body: A shorter day climbing the glacial Sarrios valley over a string of passes — Escalé, Toronez d'as Cabretas, Bernera at 2,102 m — past the Ibón de Estanés to the staffed Lizara refuge. The one to watch is Toronez d'as Cabretas in fog: follow the signs carefully so you don't drop toward the lake, which is walled off on that side.

gr11-s11.heading: Overview
gr11-s11.body: High alpine walking over the Collado del Bozo and Collado de Esper (2,262 m), past Tuca Blanca, before descending the ski slopes into Candanchú. The crux is the Paso de la Oreja, a grassy strip suspended above a drop with a fixed rope — straightforward in the dry, but treat it with real care if there's any snow.

gr11-s12.heading: Overview
gr11-s12.body: A long day, nearly 24 km, past the Canal Roya refuge and up to the Ibones de Anayet at 2,225 m beneath the Lecherines, then a long drop into Sallent de Gállego. The thing to time is the Culivillas ravine on the descent — the stream can be hard to cross during snowmelt — and the lake section can lose its markings under late snow.

gr11-s13.heading: Overview
gr11-s13.body: The shortest stage of the stretch but relentlessly uphill: a steady 900 m climb up the Aguas Limpias valley, through the Paso del Onso cliffs and past waterfalls into the Ordesa-Viñamala reserve, finishing at the Respomuso dam and refuge near 2,200 m. No real technical difficulty — just pace the continuous ascent, and book the refuge ahead in summer.

gr11-s14.heading: Overview
gr11-s14.body: The high point of the whole section: a climb past the Llena Cantal and Bachimaña lakes over the Collado de Tebarray (2,771 m), then a steep gorge descent through pine to Balneario de Panticosa. Tebarray is the crux — unstable rock and a final 40° pitch that can hold snow until mid-August, so carry an ice axe and check conditions before committing.

gr11-s15.heading: Overview
gr11-s15.body: A long, big-feeling day over the Cuello Alto de Brazato (2,566 m), with the Vignemale massif in view, past the Brazato and Batanes lakes and down into the broad Ara valley to San Nicolás de Bujaruelo. Brazato can hold snow into July and may want an ice axe early in the season — otherwise it's the climb and the 7-plus hours that ask the most of you.

gr11-s16.heading: Overview
gr11-s16.body: Down the Bujaruelo valley and through the Garganta de los Navarros gorge — including the Paso de la Escala, an airy ledge fitted with a safety line — then up the whole Ordesa valley past the Cola de Caballo waterfall to Góriz. The long, steady climb through the national park is the heart of the day; the Paso de la Escala is the one spot that demands care.

gr11-s17.heading: Overview
gr11-s17.body: From Góriz over Collado de Añisclo, then the long way down to Pineta — about 1,200m of descent in roughly 2.5km, the steepest sustained drop on the GR11, with ledges to clamber over near the bottom. The descent is the whole challenge: it is hard on the knees and slow, so leave Góriz with time in hand.

gr11-s18.heading: Overview
gr11-s18.body: A steep climb out of the Pineta cirque to the Llanos de la Larri balcony, with the north face of Monte Perdido in view, then over the Collado de las Coronetas (2,159m) and down to Parzán. No real technical difficulty — the early climb is simply long, so pace it.

gr11-s19.heading: Overview
gr11-s19.body: A long day on tracks for the climb to the Collado de Urdiceto (2,314m), past its dam and power station, then a comfortable descent through changing valleys to Viadós (Refugio de Biadós). It is the distance and 1,500m-plus of ascent that make it demanding, not the terrain — the route is well waymarked throughout.

gr11-s20.heading: Overview
gr11-s20.body: Up to the Puerto de Chistau (2,592m), with a row of 3,000m peaks across the valley, then a long descent through the Estós valley past Refugio de Estós to Puente de San Jaime. Water is easy with streams all the way; the main concern is snow lingering on the pass in early summer.

gr11-s21.heading: Overview
gr11-s21.body: Ten easy kilometres along the río Ésera to Coronas, then high-mountain ground where the path fades among boulders on the climb to the Cuello de Vallibierna (2,710m), with Aneto and the Maladeta in view. The boulder-field upper section is the crux — snow can hold there into early summer, and Coronas is a sensible bailout if conditions turn.

gr11-s22.heading: Overview
gr11-s22.body: A climb to the Collada dels Estanyets to start, then mostly downhill past the Anglios lakes and through the Salenques beech forest to Conangles, crossing from Aragon into Catalonia at the Noguera Ribagorzana. The opening climb is the only real effort; the rest is a long, scenic descent.

gr11-s23.heading: Overview
gr11-s23.body: A short stage on good paths over Port de Rius (2,355m), passing Estany de Rius before the final climb to the Restanca dam, where the refuge sits at 2,010m. Streams and lakes run the whole way, so water is never a worry — the only fuzzy part is the less-marked pull up to the refuge at the end.

gr11-s24.heading: Overview
gr11-s24.body: Seven kilometres but two high cols — Port d'Oelhacrestada (2,475m), with Montardo rising beside it, then Port de Caldes (2,568m) — dropping into the lake-strewn Circ de Colomèrs. It is short and spectacular but books out: reserve Colomèrs well ahead in July and August, and expect snow on the cols in early summer.

gr11-s25.heading: Overview
gr11-s25.body: An easy lake-to-lake stage over Port de Ratera (2,534m) on ground shared with the Carros de Foc circuit, descending past Estany de Ratera to Sant Maurici under the cliffs of Els Encantats. The one thing to watch is lingering snow on Port de Ratera early in the season; otherwise this is one of the most scenic and forgiving days on the trail.

gr11-s26.heading: Overview
gr11-s26.body: A long descent away from the national park — leaving Sant Maurici under Els Encantats and dropping over 1,200m through Espot to La Guingueta on the Noguera Pallaresa. The walking is straightforward; the day is about losing height and the knees, and trading high lakes for valley villages.

gr11-s27.heading: Overview
gr11-s27.body: A quiet, climbing stage through Dorve and over Coll de Montcaubo into remote Pallars Sobirà, past abandoned villages and barns well off the tourist line. It is solitary and steep in places — carry water, as the route stays away from reliable sources for long stretches.

gr11-s28.heading: Overview
gr11-s28.body: A gentler valley day through pine and old pastoral country, passing the Nibrós barns and other abandoned settlements on the way to Tavascan. The terrain is less rugged than the days either side; the main thing is the long, steady rhythm of a transitional stage between villages.

gr11-s29.heading: Overview
gr11-s29.body: A big six-hour day climbing out of Tavascan — the first pull above the village is brutal and eroded, with chains in places — over Boldís and Coll de Tudela toward Vall Ferrera and Àreu, under the emblematic Pica d'Estats. Pace the early climb and filter water carefully, as fountains low down can run cloudy.

gr11-s30.heading: Overview
gr11-s30.body: A demanding climb up the Vall Ferrera through streams, lakes and high meadows that give way to rock as you near Refugi de Baiau, the small hut in its amphitheatre of 2,900m peaks. It gains over 1,300m with almost no descent, so it is all uphill — arrive with enough left for the next morning's port.

gr11-s31.heading: Overview
gr11-s31.body: The crossing into Andorra over Port de Baiau (2,757m) — steep, loose boulders and scree where an ice axe and crampons may be obligatory in early summer — then a long 1,900m descent past Estany Negre and under Comapedrosa to Arans. The port is the defining challenge of the whole section: check conditions and start early.

gr11-s32.heading: Overview
gr11-s32.body: A quieter Andorran day over Coll d'Ordino, through pine forest and small villages with a steep descent into Encamp, passing the Romanesque chapel of Sant Romà de les Bons. After the high passes this is a recovery stage, though the contrast of ski-resort development in the valley is jarring.

gr11-s33.heading: Overview
gr11-s33.body: A long 20km climb up the Madriu-Perafita-Claror valley — a UNESCO World Heritage pastoral landscape of torrents, stone tracks and summer settlements — to Refugi de l'Illa beside its reservoir. The gradient is steady rather than steep; the day is about distance and the slow gain into one of Andorra's protected high valleys.

gr11-s34.heading: Overview
gr11-s34.body: A high traverse that never drops below 2,000m, crossing the Vallcivera and Engorgs cols out of Andorra into the Cerdanya toward Refugi de Malniu. Short by the standards of this section, it stays exposed and alpine — watch for afternoon heat and thinning water as the ground turns drier on the southern side.

gr11-s35.heading: Overview
gr11-s35.body: A gentle valley-floor day off the high mountain, dropping from the Malniu refuge through Guils de Cerdanya into Puigcerdà on the wide floor of the Cerdanya. The walking is easy; what matters is that this is a resupply and transport hub, a good place to restock before the climb back toward Núria.

gr11-s36.heading: Overview
gr11-s36.body: A transition stage across the Cerdanya from Puigcerdà to Planoles on tracks and quiet paths, much of it the old pilgrim route once used to reach Núria. Undemanding underfoot — the point is repositioning for the big climb that follows.

gr11-s37.heading: Overview
gr11-s37.body: A hard climbing day: about 1,440m of ascent through steep pine forest, down to Queralbs, then up through dramatic gorges past the Cua de Cavall waterfall to the sanctuary of Núria. The accumulated elevation is the challenge, so pace the long final ascent.

gr11-s38.heading: Overview
gr11-s38.body: A classic high-mountain crossing: roughly 1,000m of ascent from Núria to the Collado de Noucreus at 2,790m, then a ridge walk along the frontier crest below peaks near 2,800m to the Ulldeter refuge. Snow lingers here into late June, when an ice axe and crampons can be needed on the col.

gr11-s39.heading: Overview
gr11-s39.body: The long descent off the high Pyrenees, leaving the alpine headwaters of the Ter for the greener Ripollès valleys and dropping through Setcases down to Molló. Mostly downhill and straightforward — the day marks the real change from high mountain to mid-mountain country.

gr11-s40.heading: Overview
gr11-s40.body: A varied stage out of Molló into warmer, Mediterranean-influenced hills, with a clear shift in landscape as you enter the Alta Garrotxa toward the hamlet of Talaixà. The mid-mountain woodland and changing vegetation are the day's character; water becomes something to plan around from here on.

gr11-s41.heading: Overview
gr11-s41.body: A long descending day of about 19km through the Alta Garrotxa, passing the Romanesque chapel of Sant Aniol d'Aguja, climbing to Coll Roig and Coll de Bassegoda below the Garrotxa's highest peak, then dropping to Albanyà. Heat is the main difficulty and water is unreliable after Sant Aniol — carry enough.

gr11-s42.heading: Overview
gr11-s42.body: A day through the wooded hills of the Alt Empordà from Albanyà via Maçanet de Cabrenys to La Vajol. Forest tracks and quiet country roads make for steady walking; the interest is the villages and the increasingly Mediterranean feel rather than any single climb.

gr11-s43.heading: Overview
gr11-s43.body: The trail enters the Albera range, crossing from La Vajol via La Jonquera to the sanctuary of Mare de Déu de Requessens, set in forested hills below the ruined medieval castle. Cork oak and pine woods define the walking, and the remote sanctuary makes a quiet end to the day.

gr11-s44.heading: Overview
gr11-s44.body: A short stage on dirt tracks through the oak-lined Albera, leaving Requessens for Vilamaniscle with the first real sweeping views of the Mediterranean opening ahead. Easy underfoot — the sea coming into sight is the moment that matters.

gr11-s45.heading: Overview
gr11-s45.body: A descent out of the hills to the coast at Llançà, then along to El Port de la Selva, with the Romanesque monastery of Sant Pere de Rodes high on the Serra de Rodes above the route. The sea is the reward; the optional climb to the monastery is the day's highlight if you have the legs.

gr11-s46.heading: Overview
gr11-s46.body: The finish: about 15km along the coast and over the wind-scoured rock of the Cap de Creus headland, past coves and the strange tramuntana-eroded stone to the lighthouse and the trail's end where the Pyrenees meet the Mediterranean. Easy grades but exposed and waterless — the only water is at the lighthouse bar, so carry your own.

objectiveGuide.heading: Where to sleep
objectiveGuide.body: The trail averages roughly one refugio a day. Staffed huts are the backbone — dormitory beds, a hot meal, a packed lunch, blankets & local information; reckon on around €26 for the bed, €22 for dinner and €11 for breakfast, and bring a sleeping-bag liner since bags aren't supplied. In valleys you'll also find albergues, pensions, hostals & casas rurales, and unstaffed free huts cover the more isolated stretches. Book ahead from mid-July to late August when refuges fill fast; solo walkers can often find a space without it. Wild camping is illegal, but bivouacking — a small tent pitched at dusk and struck at dawn, away from habitation — is tolerated, usually above 1,800–2,100 m; note that camping is banned in Aigüestortes and is restricted in Ordesa to the area by the Góriz refuge.

objectiveGuide.heading: Water on the trail
objectiveGuide.body: Water is dependable across most of the GR11 — village fountains, taps near refuges & mountain springs let you refill often, and unstaffed huts usually have drinking water. The exception is the far eastern end in Catalonia, approaching the Mediterranean, where sources thin out and temperatures can top 30°C; carry extra through there. Treat what you collect: much of the easily reached water sits below grazing land and free-range cattle, so a filter or other treatment is strongly advised. Two to three litres of capacity suits most stages, with more on the dry Catalan stretches.

objectiveGuide.heading: Staying safe
objectiveGuide.body: The main risk is afternoon weather: storms build over the high country in summer, often in the evening and night, so cross exposed passes early and be down before they hit. The GR11 climbs many passes with scree, boulderfields & easy scrambling — about as hard as an inexperienced walker would want under a heavy pack — and snow can linger on passes above 2,500 m into early summer, hiding markers and demanding an ice axe and crampons. Fog can drop visibility to a few metres even on a marked route. The emergency number Spain-wide is 112, reaching mountain rescue, but reception is patchy in remote valleys, so leave your route and expected arrival with someone, and use the staffed refuges as shelter and a source of current conditions.

objectiveGuide.heading: From beech to bare granite
objectiveGuide.body: The GR11 climbs through the same vegetation belts again and again as it crosses each massif: oak and beech woods low down, the great Irati forest in Navarre, then conifers thinning out around 2,300 m, alpine meadow above that, and exposed rock above roughly 2,500 m. Watch the high ground for izards (the Pyrenean chamois, sarrio in Spanish) on the slopes and marmots whistling from boulder fields — both common above the treeline. Overhead you'll see griffon and Egyptian vultures, golden eagles, and, with luck over Ordesa y Monte Perdido, the bearded vulture or quebrantahuesos — the "bone-breaker," with a near three-metre wingspan, that lives almost entirely on bone and has its European stronghold here.

objectiveGuide.heading: Eat the valley you're in
objectiveGuide.body: Mountain food on the GR11 is shepherd's food, and it changes region by region. In the Basque Country and Navarre look for sheep's-milk cheeses — Idiazabal, and the Navarrese curados — and river trout (trucha) off the menu in valley villages. In Aragon, migas reward the hungry: stale bread crumbled and fried with garlic, chorizo, and bacon, sometimes with grapes — a meal built for a working day in the hills. Refuge dinners are usually a fixed three-course menú; stock up on cheese and cured meat in the larger valley towns like Jaca, since long stretches between resupply are normal.

objectiveGuide.heading: Drier as you go east, storms in the afternoon
objectiveGuide.body: Walk the GR11 west to east and you feel the climate change with you: the Atlantic-facing Basque hills are mist-prone and green, while by the Mediterranean end rain is rare and afternoons can climb past 30°C. July to September is the season — the high passes are usually clear of snow and the days long. The summer pattern to plan around is the thunderstorm: hot, settled mornings that build cloud through the day, with storms most often breaking in the late afternoon, evening, or overnight. Start early, aim to be off the high cols by early afternoon, and treat a clear dawn as the time to make your distance.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.title: Cabo Higuer: the Atlantic start
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: The GR11 begins at Cabo Higuer on the Gipuzkoan coast with the Cantabrian Sea at your feet, climbing inland past Hondarribia and San Marcial's chapel before crossing into Navarre at Bera.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.title: The Irati Forest and Irabia reservoir
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: East of Burguete the trail threads the western edge of the Selva de Irati, one of Europe's largest beech-fir forests, crossing the river Irati on a medieval bridge near Orbara and passing close to the Irabia reservoir.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.title: Sanctuary of Muskilda above Ochagavía
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: The descent into Ochagavía/Otsagabia, one of Navarre's prettiest mountain villages, passes the hilltop Sanctuary of Muskilda (1,020m) through the Arburua beech wood off the Abodi ridge.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.title: Elizondo and the Baztan valley
gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: The red-stone capital of the Baztan valley, Elizondo opens onto one of the wildest, best-preserved corners of the Navarran mountains, with dense beech forests climbing toward the Urkiaga pass.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.title: Aguas Tuertas valley
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: One of the most beautiful valleys in the Pyrenees, a wide glacial meadow at about 1,620 m where the Aragón Subordán river meanders in lazy zigzags past terrain rich in megalithic remains, including a dolmen.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.title: Cuello del Infierno and the Ibones Azules
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: Above Respomuso the route climbs to the Collado de Tebarray (about 2,771 m), the section's highest point, beneath the 3,082 m Picos del Infierno, then drops through the Piedrafita gorge past the blue tarns of the Ibones Azules.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.title: Refugio de Respomuso
gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: A staffed high-mountain refuge at about 2,200 m beside the Respomuso reservoir, the standard overnight above Sallent de Gállego and the launch point for the Infierno passes.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.title: Collado de Añisclo and the Pineta cirque
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: From Refugio de Góriz the GR11 climbs over the grassy Collado de Añisclo at 2,453 m before plunging into the Circo de Pineta, a vast glacial amphitheatre walled by the north face of Monte Perdido.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.title: Refugio de Góriz (2,200 m)
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: The only mountain hut inside Ordesa y Monte Perdido National Park, Góriz sits at 2,200 m and is reachable solely on foot, making it the classic base beneath Monte Perdido and the staging point for this whole section.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.title: Llanos de La Larri balcony
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: On the climb out of Pineta the trail reaches the Llanos de La Larri, a hanging natural balcony from which the full magnitude of the north face of Monte Perdido is revealed.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.title: Vallibierna lakes beneath Aneto
gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: The high stage to Refugio de Cap de Llauset passes the twin Vallibierna ibones (2,428 m and 2,475 m) under the granite giants of Aneto (3,404 m), Tempestades and Margalida.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.title: Estany de Sant Maurici & Els Encantats
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: The descent from Port de Ràtera (2,534m) ends at Estany de Sant Maurici (1,915m), set at the foot of the twin rocky spires of Els Encantats, the iconic image of the national park.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.title: Circ de Colomèrs lake country
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: Above La Restanca the trail crosses into the Circ de Colomèrs, one of the largest concentrations of glacial lakes in the Pyrenees, threading past Cap de Port, Port de Caldes and Lac Major de Colomèrs.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.title: Montardó from Oelhacrestada
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: From the Oelhacrestada col (2,475m) a short side-trip of about 40 minutes summits Montardó (2,833m), the iconic peak presiding over the Aigüestortes lakes.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.title: Coll de Tudela & Pica d'Estats panorama
gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: On the long climb out of Tavascán toward Àreu, the wide Coll de Tudela (2,239m) opens views across the Vallferrera to Pica d'Estats (3,143m), Catalonia's highest peak.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.title: Vall de Núria sanctuary
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: The GR11 climbs to the Santuari de Núria, a Marian pilgrimage shrine at 1,967m set in a glacial cirque and reached only on foot or by a historic rack railway up from Queralbs.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.title: Coll de Noucreus frontier ridge
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: Between Núria and Ulldeter the trail rides the France-Spain frontier ridge to the Collado de Noucreus at 2,790m, threading a string of 2,800m peaks like Pic de la Fossa del Gegant (2,808m) and the Vaca summits.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.title: Refugi d'Ulldeter and the source of the Ter
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: The Refugi d'Ulldeter sits at 2,220m beneath Bastiments (2,881m), Gra de Fajol and Pic de la Dona, right beside the headwaters of the river Ter.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.title: Sant Aniol d'Aguja in the Alta Garrotxa
gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: Near the end the route drops into the Alta Garrotxa's dense Mediterranean woodland of evergreen and cork oaks to the remote Romanesque chapel of Sant Aniol d'Aguja (446m), with a drinking fountain and an ideal camping spot beside the old ruined refuge.

gr11-basque-country-navarre.body: On the Hiriberri-to-Ochagavía leg over the Abodi ridge, take care in fog not to lose the waymarks between Paso Tapla and the Paso de las Alforjas, where the open high ground offers few landmarks.
Plan your water: the Ochagavía-to-Isaba stage has no drinking fountains until almost the very end, so fill up before leaving the village.
Livestock-guardian dogs (mastines) on the open pastures can be aggressive near their flocks and vipers bask in sunny rocky spots, so give dogs a wide, calm berth and watch where you step.

gr11-aragonese-pyrenees.body: The Collado de Tebarray (about 2,771 m) above Respomuso can hold snow into mid-August, with a final pitch near 40 degrees where an ice axe is advised in early-season conditions; cables are installed on the steepest sections.
Aguas Tuertas ends at a free, unstaffed refuge sleeping only 4-5 people with closed facilities onward, so carry a sleeping bag, mat and food for the next day rather than relying on services.

On the high open passes around Toronez and Bernera, navigate carefully in fog to avoid an unintended descent toward Estanés, and expect some not-always-easy stream crossings such as the Barranco de la Rueda.

gr11-ordesa-high-country.body: The Góriz–Pineta stage ends in what guides call probably the toughest descent on the whole GR11 — roughly 1,200 m of drop in about 2.5 km over ledges where you must use your hands, so wet rock or fatigue here is genuinely dangerous.

The Cuello de Vallibierna pass tops out at 2,710 m and can still hold snow in early summer, with the boulder field below the pass swallowing the path — a serious problem in fog or with a snowpack masking the route.

This is high, remote, and committing terrain: several stages cross passes above 2,300–2,710 m with long climbs (Parzán–Biados gains nearly 1,600 m over 21.7 km) and few services between huts, so build in afternoon-storm margins and don't rely on resupply between refuges.

gr11-andorra-pallars-high-country.body: In early summer you may hit lingering snow on the high cols of the Colomèrs section, between Port de Oelhacrestada (2,475m) and the stage high point at Port de Caldes (2,568m), and at Port de Ràtera (2,534m) above Sant Maurici.

The Tavascán-to-Àreu leg is a long, demanding stage climbing about 1,225m over 14.7km to Coll de Tudela, where slippery ground can be treacherous and the wooded final descent has confusing track crossings.

gr11-eastern-pyrenees.body: The high frontier traverse from Núria over Coll de Noucreus (2,790m) to Ulldeter stays above 2,700m for hours and can hold snow into early summer, where the guide warns an ice axe and crampons could be necessary.

In the closing Alta Garrotxa stage to Albanyà, water becomes complicated to find after the Sant Aniol fountain, and the next fountain at Can Nou may be dry, so fill up before the long descent through the woods.
